column60:
Emotionally, "my roommate moved out, so I have the place to myself, it's so much better for my mental health, able to workout at home, sleeping better, and cooking more, and I've been painting." 

Continues to utilize the Dexedrine appropriately, up to 40 mg/day, maximum. Continues to take 2-3 days off of this medication per week, typically. CURES reviewed, profile is not showing at this time. No evidence of diversion or misuse. 

Endorses some nights with difficulty sleeping, 'better since the roommate is gone, consistently 6-7 hours.' States energy levels are satisfactory, as evidence y cooking more, doing pilates. Seems that exercise has been good for both anxiety and sleep, notices utilization of gabapentin less frequently. 

Boyfriend is visiting this weekend, very excited about this. Residency ends in June, plan is to be reunited back in Chicago. Denying hopelessness, worthlessness, despair, helplessness. Denies anhedonia. 

Would like to establish care with a therapist, seeking one out actively at this time. However, feels she is doing better recently, handling things well without this additional support. 

States that historically, depression manifests as poor self esteem, decreased focus, increase in internal self-critic, increase in anxiety. States that she has never suffered from a severe depressive episode, though has found ways to manage with depression throughout life, including reaching out to support system, focusing on task at hand. 

No changes in health status since last assessment. 

Denies changes to appetite or energy level. Concentration is improved since re-initiation of stimulant medication. 

Denies any discrete periods with decreased need for sleep, euphoria or grandiosity or irritability, flights of idea, impulsivity, uncharacteristic talkativeness or rapid speech.

Over the past several weeks, feels anxiety has been persistent, but more motivating than debilitating. More manageable. No panic attacks. Will continue to monitor. 

Denies any history of eating disorder, though appetite sometimes is impaired with stimulant medication, but safeguards are in place related to the same (shakes, etc). States that she could be hydrating more frequently, has been getting sufficient time outside, though has not had ability to incorporate movement into daily life at this time (however, does walk to class, etc). 

Has been medicated successfully on Dexedrine, helps with sustained attention, organization, working memory. Also utilized behavioral mechanism to help regulate the same, including schedule, structure, and healthy habits/organization.

I have had Intermittent anxiety issues throughout adulthood, although I didn’t have the language to address this until seeking help from a therapist after my mother’s death. I will be ok for a while and let things build up, and then my inability to manage my feelings manifests in antisocial/angry behavior and overindulging in food and alcohol. 
COVID exacerbated all of the above, adding isolation and additional stress at work. My employer instituted a behavioral health benefit during COVID, and I began seeing a therapist/psychiatrist in summer of 2020. He retired about a year ago, but I realize that I need more help. What I find useful are strategies and tools to counteract my negative thought patterns and examples of behavior I can follow to change how I respond to stress and negativity. I am currently not using alcohol or drugs except escitalopram, which has helped a lot. I tried a meditation/mindfulness class, which wasn’t very useful. I really like yoga and try to practice 5-6 days per week

column59: I need help. I have zero energy and want to have some what of a life. I need help.
column60: in 1987 I began having depression and mania kind of. I had reported my sexual abuse by my grandfather and uncle so I thought it was just from that. I went to college met a boy and used work as my stability. After my Mom passed in 2003 in 8 weeks and I had a 8 month old and my  year old I had a mental break. I was not hospitalized but only because my husband at the time was able to step in and totally support me. I was on mood stabilzers and other meds including ativan as needed for years then in 2010 I was 40 I was diagnosed with Hypothyoid and eventally Hashimotos. It took years but then I got my thyroid balanced went gluten free and was very stabalized. In 2014 I got cancer, got divorced and drank alcohol for 2 years, now sober  years and had not drank in the past during my marriage of 14 years. I got sober and my anxiety  and mood stuff came back I did a partial hospitalization program got stable with seroquel, maybe 5 ativan per month for panic attacks. I tried vraylar, prozac, well butrin, depakote again but it just was not doing the trick. I ended up going in higher dose seroquel and it was like magic for me. I finished a medical assisting program and graduated from college with my associates degree. I ended up working as a psychiatric medical assistant at a PHP program (ironic) then covid happen and I was also working at a company called B 12 love for fun. When Covid happened I had no idea which job would pan out. I have been permantely disbled for Bipolar 1 since 2003 and tried my best to work full time and go off disability. I did it for 3.5 years but then on October 29, 2022 I had to go back off work because I had hit my max mental load. I had transfered to my B12 love job and left the mental health clinic after 18 months. I thought working at my "fun" job was the soulution. I became the manager of a clinic and started on salary and I began working 75 hours a week and dealing with people very sick from covid coming to natural medicine to get help. I became too stressed and had another break no hospitization and by luck disability started my benefit right away. I am still permantely disable and not collecting my benefits again. While I was working like crazy my doctor put me on bubernorphine for what he thought was back pain, while I was off work I find out I had hip dysplaia and had a hip replacement. I went off Belbucca on my own and then I went off oxycodone also. I am drinking 9 shots of expreesson per day and now have been diagnosed with ehler danlos hyper mobility and have chronic fatigue and pain. COMORBID BIPOLAR and ADHD:
Adult ADHD is highly comorbid with mood disorders, when compared with the general population OR=2.7 to 7.5 (95% CI 3.0-8.2). It is thus not unusual for patients, such as this one, to have concurrent ADHD and bipolar disorder diagnoses. While we recognize that psychostimulants could potentially exacerbate symptoms of bipolar disorder, after weighing risks, benefits, and alternatives, it was determined that the benefits of continuing psychostimulants outweigh the potential risks at this time. These risks are mitigated by close monitoring of the patient, prescription of lowest necessary doses, concurrent prescription of mood stabilizing agents.

I don’t want to destabilize, I have been able to do the work and put my life together because of it. I wouldn’t chance going off my meds alone, but maybe lowering doses supervised". column59: Recommended by my PCP.  I have a history of depression, though that has largely been in check for the past few years through medication and coping mechanisms.  But my anxiety is at an all-time high, and I really want to discuss ADHD, as I've gotten worse and worse at starting tasks, which is affecting my job.
column60:
Depression: Had suicidal ideation from ages 16-22, but outside of a few relapses due to life stressors, haven't gotten back to that in a long time.
Anxiety: Once the depression started to subside, it was replaced by general anxiety. At one point during law school, it peaked to the point of debilitating panic attacks, so I was briefly on klonopin for these emergency situations. But it has made me change my life in negative ways- I recently quit a comedy class (and lost out on $500) because I was having panic attacks about going in. In some situations, I've taken to abusing drugs/alcohol to overcome that hump, which obviously can be dangerous. 
ADHD: This has always been something I've thought about addressing, but the two above have seemed more important to hit first.  But I'm a lawyer/consultant, and in my personal time I write and perform comedy. I'm ostensibly an outgoing, high-achieving person, but in reality I find it so, so hard to start anything, until I'm up to (or past) a hard deadline. Most of my day is filled with sitting at my desk, bouncing my legs, thinking about doing twenty different things, but not starting anything. My apartment that I share with my partner is a mess, and it's caused issues in our relationship. I actually enjoy the mechanical process of cleaning, but I feel the need to let it stack up until it's a big project, rather than keeping things clean regularly. I've had success with a script I wrote, but I've gotten about 5 pages into my next script in the course of 3 months due to putting it off, which is making my opportunities to actually pursue writing wither; no one wants to take on a writer who writes a script every 3 years. I'm beginning to feel like if I don't address this quickly, at age 32, I'm starting to get to the point where it gets harder to shift my career in a way that I want to.

column65:
I have very little family history, as my biological father isn't in the picture, and my mom was adopted. My mom ended up finding her birth family, and her mother (my maternal grandmother) committed suicide shortly after giving my mom up for adoption. Even though I've been open about my mental health with her in the past, my mom isn't with hers, but I've heard from my sister that she's had a Xanax script for years. 

My two half-sisters both have been treated for depression; my oldest half-sister (7 years younger than me) just finished her PsyD and is looking to work in mental health; suffice it to say, we've had a vested interest in mental health.
column66:
Born and raised in Upstate NY; originally to a single mom who married when I was 6.  Largely raised by my maternal grandparents, a lot of family trauma that I've unpacked over years of therapy. Never physical abuse, but years of incredibly direct emotional abuse. I've been fully independent since 18, although I've allowed some contact with my parents. 

I have a JD, and am a member of the State Bar of California; I also passed the NY Bar, but never registered. Currently working in regulatory consulting, but am desperate for a career change to something more personally fulfilling. 

I'm in a committed long-term relationship; we met in NY in 2016, but only made it official about 2 years ago. We moved in together last February. Him and I may move back to NYC at some point. Outside of that, I have a very solid group of friends here in LA, and I've recently been able to start expanding it outside of friends-of-friends, by joining comedy and writing groups. 

Current leisure/hobbies- mostly creative, via writing or live comedy. I have a Dungeons & Dragons group with a few other comedians that meets monthly. Also a lifelong gamer (and my partner works in gaming) so that's a big chunk of my spare time. I used to be much more physically active (in the past I've done Crossfit, OrangeTheory, played in the NYC LGBT Softball League, the LA Gay Bowling League) but currently don't have a physical outlet other than at-home workouts. Looking to do something soon, but I feel like I'm hesitant due to my current cardio-ability and body image. Keep telling myself that once I get back to 270, I'll be more outgoing, but that hasn't happened. 

Current stressors: as above; hate my career, and my body. I had to put my artistic side away early in life when it became clear that I had no family support system, so I did the "right thing" and got a good degree, which lead to a high-paying career. But my personal political/philosophical background is, suffice it to say, not aligned with my current job, which is primarily making sure rich American capitalists stay rich. So I'm desperate to move to something that incorporates both my legal/finance background, and my artistic side, but also want to maintain our current quality-of-life, which is obviously difficult. 

For my body- even when I was down to 228, I wasn't necessarily happy with how I looked, but getting back up to where I am now is really tough. I started Semaglutide and the lower doses at the start have been fine, but I haven't really dedicated myself to focusing on diet and exercise as much as I need to.

column75:
Alcohol- regular, but not daily. Probably 6 drinks per week, but usually between Thurs-Sat. Occasional social binges. 
Cannabis- Used to be heavy, but a couple years ago it started triggering anxiety really badly (especially edibles), so I've tapered off. An occasional joint of lower-THC flower here or there. 
Cocaine- I had truthfully never used it before 2020 (it felt like I was one of the only ones in law school not using!), but since moving to LA, it has become an occasional party thing. I did have a run of a couple months where I was using it to get motivated to get work done, which is definitely part of why I wanted to reach out, to find a healthier alternative. 
Psilocybin- a handful of times in the past 5 years. Had two bad experiences that caused acute suicidal ideation, so I've not tried again. 
MDMA- also an occasional party drug, maybe four times per year or so. 

I've had maybe six cigarettes in my life and have never vaped; and no other drugs than those listed above.

column59: i'm so tired of feeling like my brain is working against me. I get emotionally overwhelmed by the smallest things and it's so hard to come out from under. my anxiety has taken many forms and currently it has me questioning my relationship even though I want it to work out so badly (i'm engaged and just bought a house with my partner and don't want to blow up my life). I also have hEDs and all the health anxiety that comes with that. I feel like I don't know how to thrive, I'm just stuck in survival mode.
column60:
I suppose I've always had anxiety even though I didn't know it. In my early 20s I had a terrible binge eating disorder...tons of guilt and shame that came with that - still haven't been able to shake it. Shame is a big part of my inner talk. I went on adderall for ADHD in my mid-20s and thought I had found the golden ticket. I was able to focus and not get overwhelmed so easily and I was genuinely enjoying life. Probably the happiest I've been aside from being a young child. However, I began to feel immense fear and guilt for being on a medication (being reliant on something outside myself) and I began experiencing weird health symptoms. I was diagnosed hEDs in my early 20s but didn't experience symptoms consciously until my early 30s. I got off adderall and felt like i was taking control of my health and enjoyed the calm/non stimulated feeling of being off adderall. However, since then I've just been so freaking confused. Why is my health doing what its doing? Is it my fault? Did I mess everything up somehow? I can't trust my life choices since then because I don't feel like i'm standing on solid ground. I got engaged during this time and that has added a ton of anxiety - even though the relationship gives me a lot of comfort. I just feel so lost and uninspired and like i'm drowning. I have intense emotional reactions to the littlest things and it makes me feel crazy. I feel like I am so smart and have so much potential to do amazing things and yet I just hide away and do nothing because I'm in a cage. I feel like i'm in a physical and emotional cage and I'm not sure how I got in and I don't know how to get out.

Currently working as a senior experience designer. "I create branding and designer."
Currently engaged to Steve, met online 3 years ago, live together, just bought a house. He is incredibly easy to get along with and wonderful."
